food_subgroup: Stir-fried vegetable and grain side dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried corn; closest parent 'corn food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for cooked maize used as closest parent; no specific stir-fried corn entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried corn as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate of stir-fried corn kernels as typically served in a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Corn, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 20014 / FDC ID 169998); adjusted for stir-frying with oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values from USDA SR Legacy cooked yellow corn (FDC ID 169998). Fat and energy adjusted upward to account for stir-frying with approximately 5 g cooking oil per 100 g serving, consistent with standard stir-fry preparation. Sodium adjusted to reflect light seasoning. All other macros retained from the USDA source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Corn, sweet, boiled (GI = 52)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweet corn (boiled). Stir-fried corn is prepared from the same base ingredient with minimal processing change; GI is expected to remain in the low-medium range. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 21.0 g carbohydrate × 200 g serving) / (100 × 100) = 21.8 g. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried corn was found in the approved GI database.

hypertension.guidance: Stir-fried corn contains approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt and seasoning; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one small serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 and a glycemic load of 21.8 per standard 200 g serving, stir-fried corn can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (100–120 g) and pair it with protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and presence of dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried corn is relatively low in saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g) and provides 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which supports cardiovascular health; however, the added cooking oil contributes unsaturated fats that are generally cardioprotective when used in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried corn (Vietnamese: Bắp xào) is a popular Vietnamese-style side dish consisting of sweet corn kernels rapidly cooked in a hot wok or frying pan with cooking oil, garlic, spring onion, and light seasoning such as salt and sometimes butter. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant in Singapore, reflecting the Vietnamese culinary tradition of using simple, fresh ingredients with high-heat wok cooking to achieve a lightly caramelised, tender-crisp texture with a naturally sweet flavour from the corn. Nutritionally, stir-fried corn provides moderate carbohydrates (approximately 21 g per 100 g), modest protein (3.4 g), and dietary fibre (2.4 g), along with B vitamins including thiamine and niacin, and small amounts of vitamin C and potassium. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low-medium range), making it a moderate glycemic food. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size due to the moderate glycemic load, and those with hypertension should be aware of added sodium from seasoning. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian-friendly, though it may contain butter and is therefore not strictly vegan or dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No exact match for stir-fried corn was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked yellow corn (FDC ID 169998) with a standard stir-fry oil adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled sweet corn. Confidence is medium due to the adaptation rather than an exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169998 (cooked yellow corn) with fat and energy adjusted for stir-frying with approximately 5 g oil per 100 g. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — corn is a plant food containing no vitamin B12 by nature; naturally zero). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — corn contains no vitamin D; naturally zero). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in this preparation; naturally zero for plant-based stir-fry with standard cooking oil). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — corn kernels are a plant food and contain no cholesterol). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from USDA oil composition data). 7. The food name in the input JSON contains a likely OCR or encoding artefact ('coỏn'); the canonical English name has been corrected to 'Stir-Fried Corn'. 8. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate for boiled sweet corn from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for stir-fried corn was identified. 9. Sodium of 180 mg per 100 g is an estimate reflecting light seasoning typical of Vietnamese stir-fry preparation. 10. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road, Singapore, and is classified as Vietnamese cuisine served in a Singapore context; multilingual names cover all five mandatory Singaporean cuisine languages plus Vietnamese.

Added all five mandatory Singaporean cuisine multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese.
